title: Advertising-consumer interaction
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Consumer — how consumers process ads: attention, comprehension, and attitude change.
learningObjectives: Analyse central vs peripheral route processing (ELM).
Evaluate mere exposure and repetition effects.
Assess ad avoidance and scepticism in consumers.

simpleExplanation.title: From ad to action
simpleExplanation.summary: Ads must grab attention, be understood, and change attitudes — but consumers often use quick peripheral cues or actively resist messages.

flashcards.back: Attention → Interest → Desire → Action — linear model of ad effectiveness from exposure to purchase.
High involvement + ability to process → evaluate ad arguments carefully → lasting attitude change (Petty & Cacioppo).
Low involvement or distraction → rely on cues (celebrity, music, attractiveness) → weaker, temporary attitude change.
Repeated exposure to stimulus increases liking — even without conscious awareness — explains ad repetition strategy.
Consumer cynicism about ad claims — reduces persuasion, especially among older/experienced consumers.
Skipping ads, using ad-blockers, changing channels — reduces exposure and campaign effectiveness.

sections.content: **Ad-consumer interaction** (2.5.2) — **Petty & Cacioppo ELM** is key for evaluation.

sections.question: A smartphone ad with detailed specification comparisons increases purchase intent among tech enthusiasts but not casual viewers. The same brand's billboard using only a celebrity and slogan increases brand liking after repeated exposure. Many viewers skip the TV version using ad-blockers. Using models of ad processing, explain these findings and evaluate the campaigns.
sections.solution:
**Spec comparison — ELM central route:** **Tech enthusiasts** = high **involvement** + **ability** to process → engage with **argument quality** (specs) → **enduring attitude change** and **purchase intent** (Petty & Cacioppo).

**Casual viewers — no effect from specs:** Low involvement → **peripheral route** only — detailed specs **ignored** (exceed motivation/ability threshold).

**Billboard — peripheral route + mere exposure:** **Celebrity + slogan** = **peripheral cues** (attractiveness, fame). **Repeated exposure** → **mere exposure effect** (Zajonc) — increased **familiarity** and **liking** without deep processing.

**Ad-blocker skipping — ad avoidance:** **Active resistance** reduces **effective reach** — tech-savvy audience **least exposed** to TV spec ad despite being highest involvement.

**Evaluate — campaign fit:** Dual strategy is **appropriate** — central route for **target segment**; peripheral + repetition for **mass awareness** (2.5.3).

**Evaluate — limitations:** **Peripheral change** is **fragile** — easily reversed by competitor ads. **Mere exposure** has **ceiling** — over-exposure causes **irritation**. **Ad avoidance** growing — **digital natives** hardest to reach via traditional TV. **Ethics** — peripheral route exploits **low-involvement** processing without informed evaluation.
